# Results

## Amelia's movement

After moderate pre processing effort, Amelia's tracking data over more than two weeks looks like in @fig-move_google. Blue are the movement points and red are the static points. As some of the trajectories have little data points, the travelled distances and speed are likely to be underestimated.

![Tracked moving data with google timeline, red is static and blue is moving.](plots/tm_movement_amelia.png){#fig-move_google width="50%"}


The other parameters are calculated once per day and once an average over the whole tracking period. In @fig-param_day_a it is shown, that the parameters vary widely at different days.

![Parameter results of average travel speed, maximal distance from home, time out of home and total travelled distance for every tracked day of Amelia.](plots/param_sum_day_amelia.png){#fig-param_day_a width="90%"}




## Christopher's movement

Christopher’s GPS Logger data shows more detailed trajectories than the Google Timeline data (@fig-move_gps). After removing stationary periods, the main movement patterns become clearly visible. Most longer trips follow railway corridors, indicating that commuting and other long-distance movements were mainly done by train. The high spatial resolution also makes local movements visible, although gaps and noisy points remain due to tunnels, app crashes and poor GPS signal indoors.


![Tracked moving data with GPS app, red is static and blue is moving.](plots/tm_movement_christopher.png){#fig-move_gps width="25%"}


The daily calculated parameter for Christopher also shows significant variation between days as shown in @fig-param_day_c.

![Parameter results of average travel speed, maximal distance from home, time out of home and total travelled distance for every tracked day of Christopher.](plots/param_sum_day_christopher.png){#fig-param_day_c width="90%"}






## Comparison

When comparing the movement behavior of Amelia and Christopher, several similarities emerge. Both travel at a similar average speed, suggesting comparable ratios of train/vehicle use versus walking. Commuting times to ZHAW are also similar on average. Amelia travels slightly more on average, both in total distance and time spent outside the home, though this is strongly influenced by a few outliers that raise the mean. Regarding maximum travel radius, Christopher appears to travel the same distance more consistently, as his mean value lies at the top of the boxplot's interquartile range.

![Comparison of movement parameters of Amelia and Christopher.](plots/param_sum_comp.png){#param_sum_comp}


By examining the road types used, the likely mode of travel can be estimated. "Rail" corresponds to train travel, "main road" typically indicates bus or passenger vehicle use (and in some cases cycling), while "local road" may correspond to walking, cycling, or driving. Comparing the time shares spent on different road types reveals clear differences between the two. Christopher appears to travel primarily by train, whereas Amelia spends more time on roads in general (note that some points may be misclassified).

![Share of time on the road types of Amelia and Christopher.](plots/road_type_pie_comp.png){#road_type_pie_comp width="90%"}
